role_summary: SNCC field organizer and voter-registration project director in Hale County, Alabama and Natchez, Mississippi.
ai_reading: Avery's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 104), the Mississippi SNCC voter-registration project director; she also clusters with C. T. Vivian (entry 134) and Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53). The cluster matches her cultural placement as a Mississippi-side SNCC field organizer. The novel observation comes from the Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision axis, where Avery sits at +0.69, far toward Strategic Vision and a position the cultural record would not predict for someone identified with concrete on-the-ground action (the 1961 Woolworth's sit-in, the Edmund Pettus Bridge crossing on Bloody Sunday, fieldwork in Hale County and Natchez). The embedding may be catching the retrospective character of her oral history, where the on-record statement about carrying weapons in Mississippi and questioning strict nonviolence reads more as strategic position-taking than tactical reportage.
biographical_paragraph: Annie Pearl Avery's SNCC assignments tracked the geography of Deep South voter registration: directing the Hale County, Alabama project, then staffing the Natchez, Mississippi office [src: 2]. Birmingham was her birthplace in 1943, but her childhood crossed back and forth across the Ohio River, the family moving north to Pittsburgh when she was about nine and returning her to Birmingham at fourteen [src: 2]. By 1961, still a teenager, she was sitting in at a Woolworth's organized by SCLC's Fred Shuttlesworth, and that same year she met James Forman and Julian Bond at a SNCC conference in Georgia [src: 2]. Avery was on the Edmund Pettus Bridge on March 7, 1965, the day Alabama troopers attacked marchers and the day passed into movement memory as Bloody Sunday [src: 2]. On the question of armed self-defense within civil rights organizing, her record is on the record: she has stated that she carried weapons while doing voter-registration work in Mississippi and that the strict-nonviolence orthodoxy was, in her view, tactically wanting [src: 2]. Entry 3 of the Civil Rights History Project at the Library of Congress is her oral history [src: 1].

sources.title: Annie Pearl Avery oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress
